---
title: "Building a Bulk Email Dispatcher in Shiny"
description: "How I built a production Shiny app that sends hundreds of personalised HTML emails via Outlook — with rate limiting, a test mode, a live progress bar, and a rendered R Markdown email template."
author: "Andrew Fuller"
date: "2026-05-20"
categories: [R, Shiny, Email, Tutorial, Production]
image: "thumbnail.png"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
---

At AROC, we send personalised data deadline reminder emails to hundreds of Australian rehabilitation hospitals
every reporting cycle. Each email contains a colour-coded summary table showing that hospital's episode
submission counts across the year — and whether they're on track, lagging, or missing data entirely.

For a long time this was a semi-manual process. It eventually got replaced with a proper Shiny
module that handles the whole workflow: previewing the campaign, test-sending to a sandbox
address, and finally dispatching to all real recipients — with a live progress bar and an
Outlook integration that respects Microsoft's sending rate limits.

This post walks through how it's built.

---

## The Problem

We needed to send roughly 200–400 emails per campaign, each one:

- **Personalised** — containing the specific hospital's own episode counts
- **Formatted as HTML** — rendered from an R Markdown template with a colour-coded `{gt}` table
- **Sent from an institutional address** — via Outlook, using `SentOnBehalfOfName`
- **Trackable** — the database needed updating after each send so we didn't double-send

The naive approach (a loop with `blastula` or `sendmailR`) hit problems fast: rate limits,
no visibility into progress, and a terrifying "send all" button with no safety net.

---

## Architecture Overview

The solution is three layers:

```
1. Plan      fn.PrepareEmailPlan()   – filter data, build a per-hospital send list
2. Execute   fn.ExecuteSendAll()     – orchestrate: show waiter, call send, hide waiter
3. Send      fn.SendAllEmails()      – the actual loop: render → send → sleep → repeat
```

The Shiny UI wraps this with a three-step workflow:

> **Calculate Summary → Test Campaign → Send Production**

Each step is gated — you can't send production until you've calculated, and you really
shouldn't until you've test-sent first.

---

## Step 1: Building the Email Plan

Before a single email goes out, `fn.PrepareEmailPlan()` assembles a row per hospital per pathway:

```r
fn.PrepareEmailPlan <- function(
  session_data,
  data_chasing_raw,
  filter_active     = TRUE,
  exclude_email_sent = FALSE,
  exclude_complete   = FALSE,
  filter_by_pathway  = FALSE,
  pathway_select     = "Inpatient",
  Paed_ids
) {

  # Combine all pathway datasets with a pathway_name column
  inpatient_data  <- session_data$dataSummaryInpatient  |> mutate(pathway_num = 3L,  pathway_name = "Inpatient")
  paed_data       <- session_data$dataSummaryPaed       |> mutate(pathway_num = if_else(Path == "Inpatient", 33L, 44L), pathway_name = paste0("Paediatric - ", Path))
  inreach_data    <- session_data$dataSummaryInreach    |> mutate(pathway_num = 2L,  pathway_name = "Inreach")
  ambulatory_data <- session_data$dataSummaryAmbulatory |> mutate(pathway_num = 4L,  pathway_name = "Ambulatory")
  rith_data       <- session_data$dataSummaryRITH       |> mutate(pathway_num = 7L,  pathway_name = "RITH")

  all_data <- bind_rows(
    inpatient_data  |> select(HospitalId, ReportIdentifier, ReportName, HospitalName, Flag_Active, Flag_EmailSent, Complete, pathway_num, pathway_name),
    paed_data       |> select(HospitalId, ReportIdentifier, ReportName, HospitalName, Flag_Active, Flag_EmailSent, Complete, pathway_num, pathway_name),
    inreach_data    |> select(HospitalId, ReportIdentifier, ReportName, HospitalName, Flag_Active, Flag_EmailSent, Complete, pathway_num, pathway_name),
    ambulatory_data |> select(HospitalId, ReportIdentifier, ReportName, HospitalName, Flag_Active, Flag_EmailSent, Complete, pathway_num, pathway_name),
    rith_data       |> select(HospitalId, ReportIdentifier, ReportName, HospitalName, Flag_Active, Flag_EmailSent, Complete, pathway_num, pathway_name)
  )

  # Apply filters based on UI controls
  filtered_data <- all_data
  if (filter_active)       filtered_data <- filtered_data |> filter(Flag_Active == 1)
  if (exclude_email_sent)  filtered_data <- filtered_data |> filter(Flag_EmailSent == FALSE | is.na(Flag_EmailSent))
  if (exclude_complete)    filtered_data <- filtered_data |> filter(Complete == FALSE | is.na(Complete))
  if (filter_by_pathway)   filtered_data <- filtered_data |> filter(pathway_name == pathway_select)

  # One row per hospital+pathway, with a list-column of all their report IDs
  email_plan <- filtered_data |>
    group_by(HospitalId, pathway_num, pathway_name) |>
    summarise(
      hospital_name       = first(HospitalName),
      report_identifiers  = list(ReportIdentifier),
      report_count        = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  # Fetch contacts and pre-build the facility data slice for the Rmd template
  email_plan <- email_plan |>
    rowwise() |>
    mutate(
      contacts      = list(data_chasing_raw |> filter(HospitalId == HospitalId)),
      to_emails     = paste(unique(contacts$Email[!is.na(contacts$Email) & contacts$Email != ""]), collapse = ","),
      contact_count = length(unique(contacts$Email[!is.na(contacts$Email) & contacts$Email != ""])),
      facility_data = list({
        ids <- report_identifiers
        df  <- switch(as.character(pathway_num),
          "3"  = session_data$dataSummaryInpatient,
          "2"  = session_data$dataSummaryInreach,
          "4"  = session_data$dataSummaryAmbulatory,
          "7"  = session_data$dataSummaryRITH,
          "33" = ,
          "44" = session_data$dataSummaryPaed,
          NULL
        )
        if (!is.null(df)) df |> filter(ReportIdentifier %in% ids) else NULL
      })
    ) |>
    ungroup()

  list(
    email_plan        = email_plan,
    summary_stats     = list(
      total_emails    = nrow(email_plan),
      unique_hospitals = length(unique(email_plan$HospitalId)),
      unique_contacts  = sum(email_plan$contact_count),
      total_effective_emails = sum(email_plan$contact_count),
      total_reports    = sum(email_plan$report_count)
    ),
    pathway_breakdown = email_plan |>
      group_by(pathway_name) |>
      summarise(emails = n(), hospitals = n_distinct(HospitalId), reports = sum(report_count), contacts = sum(contact_count), .groups = "drop")
  )
}
```

The key design choices here:

1. **`list()` columns for report IDs** — each row holds *all* a hospital's report IDs as a list, so one email covers all their reports for a pathway in a single send
2. **`facility_data` is pre-sliced** — the Rmd template receives a data frame filtered to just that hospital, not the whole dataset
3. **Contacts are fetched inline via `rowwise()`** — slightly slow for very large plans, but avoids a complex join

---

## Step 2: The Email Template

The actual email is a parameterised R Markdown document (`CreateEmail.Rmd`) that renders to HTML
and produces a colour-coded `{gt}` table.

```yaml
---
output: blastula::blastula_email
params:
  path:                    NA
  facility:                NA
  web_config_period:       NA
  web_config_filtered_months: NA
---
```

The template automatically:

- Calculates the correct **deadline date** from the reporting period name
  (`FY2025` → 14 August 2025, `CY2025` → 14 February 2026)
- Formats **month column headers** with year suffixes (`Jul-24`, `Aug-24`, …)
- Conditionally colour-codes cells: 🟥 zero count, 🟨 below lower bound, 🟩 above upper bound

```r
for (i in seq_along(web_config_filtered_months)) {
  tbl1 <- tbl1 |>
    tab_style(
      style     = list(cell_text(color = "#b2a46d", weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#ffeb9c")),
      locations = cells_body(columns = web_config_filtered_months[i],
                             rows    = tbl1$`_data`[[web_config_filtered_months[i]]] <= lower_bound)
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style     = list(cell_text(color = "#86a28c", weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#c6efce")),
      locations = cells_body(columns = web_config_filtered_months[i],
                             rows    = tbl1$`_data`[[web_config_filtered_months[i]]] >= upper_bound)
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      style     = list(cell_text(color = "#b28b90", weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#ffc7ce")),
      locations = cells_body(columns = web_config_filtered_months[i],
                             rows    = tbl1$`_data`[[web_config_filtered_months[i]]] == 0)
    )
}
```

The rendered HTML is read back in as a character string and stuffed directly into Outlook's
`HTMLbody` field — no attachment, no external hosting required.

---

## Step 3: Sending with Rate Limiting

This is the part that makes the whole system production-safe.

Microsoft's Office 365 imposes a rate limit of around 30 emails per minute for most tenants
(~1,800/hour). To stay well within limits we target **250 emails per hour**:

```r
fn.SendAllEmails <- function(
  email_plan,
  test_mode          = FALSE,
  test_email         = "fullera@uow.edu.au",
  target_rate_per_hour = 250,
  ...
) {

  sleep_per_email <- (60 * 60) / target_rate_per_hour   # seconds per email = 14.4s

  for (i in 1:nrow(email_plan)) {
    row <- email_plan[i, ]

    tryCatch({
      # Render the Rmd to HTML for this specific hospital
      rmarkdown::render(
        "CreateEmail.Rmd",
        params = list(
          path                       = row$pathway_num,
          facility                   = row$facility_data[[1]],
          web_config_period          = web_config_period,
          web_config_filtered_months = web_config_filtered_months
        ),
        quiet = TRUE
      )

      # Read the rendered HTML
      email_body <- paste(readr::read_lines("CreateEmail.html"), collapse = " ")

      # Redirect all recipients in test mode
      to_recipients <- if (test_mode) test_email else row$to_emails
      subject_line  <- paste0(
        if (test_mode) "[TEST] " else "",
        "AROC ", row$pathway_name, " data deadline for ", web_config_period,
        " at ", row$hospital_name
      )

      # Send via Outlook COM automation
      OutApp  <- RDCOMClient::COMCreate("Outlook.Application")
      outMail <- OutApp$CreateItem(0)
      outMail[["To"]]                <- gsub(",", ";", to_recipients)
      outMail[["subject"]]           <- subject_line
      outMail[["HTMLbody"]]          <- email_body
      outMail[["SentOnBehalfOfName"]] <- "aroc-enquiries@uow.edu.au"
      outMail$Send()

      # Rate limit: sleep proportional to the number of contacts for this hospital
      Sys.sleep(sleep_per_email * row$contact_count)

    }, error = function(e) {
      emails_failed <<- emails_failed + 1
    })
  }
}
```

A few things worth noting:

### Rate limiting math

The `Sys.sleep()` call at the end of each iteration is sized by `contact_count` — because
one "hospital email" might go to 3 people, which counts as 3 sends against the rate limit.

```r
Sys.sleep(sleep_per_email * row$contact_count)
# e.g. if 3 contacts: sleep 14.4s × 3 = 43.2s before next send
```

### `RDCOMClient` COM automation

`RDCOMClient` lets R drive Outlook as a COM object. It needs:
- Outlook installed on the machine running the Shiny server ✅
- The R process running as a user with Outlook configured ✅
- The `SentOnBehalfOfName` field to be an address the user has delegate access for ✅

```r
OutApp  <- RDCOMClient::COMCreate("Outlook.Application")
outMail <- OutApp$CreateItem(0)       # 0 = MailItem
outMail[["To"]]      <- "a@b.com;c@d.com"   # semicolon-separated
outMail[["HTMLbody"]] <- email_html
outMail$Send()
```

---

## Step 4: Test Mode

The test mode pattern is threaded all the way through the stack via a single `test_mode` boolean:

```r
# In the UI — confirmation dialog before test send
shinyalert(
  title = "Test Send All Emails?",
  text = tagList(
    div(class = "alert alert-info",
      h4("TEST MODE - SAFE TO SEND"),
      p(strong("ALL emails will ONLY be sent to: "), code("fullera@uow.edu.au"))
    ),
    p("✓ Subjects marked with [TEST]"),
    p("✓ NO database updates will be made")
  ),
  html = TRUE,
  type = "info",
  ...
)
```

When `test_mode = TRUE`:

1. `to_recipients` is replaced with the sandbox address regardless of what's in the plan
2. `subject_line` is prefixed with `[TEST]`
3. `fn.UpdateLog()` is **not** called — so the database flag stays `Flag_EmailSent = FALSE`

This means you can send 5 test emails to yourself, inspect them in Outlook, and then run the
real campaign knowing nothing in the database was touched.

---

## Step 5: Live Progress Feedback

The trickiest UX part: showing progress while R is blocked in a for loop.

The solution uses `waiter` for the overlay, but instead of the standard spinner we attach a
custom HTML progress bar and update it via custom JavaScript messages:

```r
# Inside the send loop — update the waiter overlay
session$sendCustomMessage(
  "updateWaiterProgress",
  list(
    current = i,
    total   = total_emails,
    percent = round((i / total_emails) * 100, 1),
    message = row$hospital_name     # "Sending to Royal Rehab..."
  )
)
```

```javascript
// JavaScript handler registered in the app's www/
Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler("updateWaiterProgress", function(data) {
  document.getElementById("waiter-progress-text").textContent =
    data.current + " / " + data.total + " emails sent";
  document.getElementById("waiter-progress-bar").style.width = data.percent + "%";
  document.getElementById("waiter-progress-message").textContent = data.message;
});
```

The waiter overlay HTML is built in `fn.CreateEmailWaiter()`:

```r
fn.CreateEmailWaiter <- function(ns_func = NULL) {
  Waiter$new(
    html = tagList(
      div(class = "waiter-content-center",
        img(src = "logo.png", style = "width: 150px;"),
        h4(id = "waiter-progress-text", "Preparing to send emails..."),
        div(class = "waiter-progress-bar-container",
          div(id = "waiter-progress-bar", class = "waiter-progress-bar",
              style = "width: 0%;")
        ),
        p(id = "waiter-progress-message", "Initializing...")
      )
    ),
    color = "rgba(255,255,255,0.9)",
    fadeout = TRUE
  )
}
```

The result: a full-screen overlay that smoothly updates with the hospital name and a percentage
bar as each email is dispatched.

---

## Estimated Wait Time

Before the user confirms the production send, the app shows them how long to expect to wait:

```r
total_effective_emails <- rv$summary_stats$total_effective_emails
seconds_per_email      <- (60 * 60) / 250          # = 14.4s
wait_seconds           <- total_effective_emails * seconds_per_email
wait_minutes           <- wait_seconds / 60

wait_time_text <- if (wait_minutes >= 60) {
  paste0(round(wait_minutes / 60, 1), " hours")
} else {
  paste0(round(wait_minutes, 1), " minutes")
}
```

If you're sending to 300 contacts, that's ~72 minutes. Better to know upfront than wonder
why the app is frozen.

---

## What I'd Do Differently

1. **Move to an SMTP-based solution** — `RDCOMClient` requires Outlook on the server, which
   is messy in a containerised deployment. `Microsoft365R` or a proper SMTP relay would be
   more portable.

2. **Run the send loop in a future/promise** — currently `fn.SendAllEmails()` blocks the R
   process. Using `future_promise()` would let the Shiny session remain responsive, though
   progress updates from a background thread require additional plumbing.

3. **Add resume-from-failure** — if the loop errors at email 150 of 200, you currently have
   to re-run from scratch (with the "exclude already sent" filter). An explicit checkpoint
   would be better.

---

## Packages Used

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| `RDCOMClient` | Outlook COM automation |
| `rmarkdown` | Render the HTML email template per hospital |
| `gt` | Colour-coded episode count table inside the email |
| `waiter` | Full-screen loading overlay during send |
| `shinyalert` | Confirmation dialogs (test vs production) |
| `shinyjs` | Disable/enable buttons during send |
| `reactable` | Campaign preview tables |
| `highcharter` | Report distribution chart |
| `dplyr` / `purrr` | Data wrangling and rowwise operations |

---

The complete module lives across `send_all_module.R` (~814 lines), `email_module.R` (~651 lines),
and `CreateEmail.Rmd` (~260 lines). What started as a scary "just loop through them all"
script turned into something I'm actually proud to run in production.

If you're building something similar and want to talk through the architecture, feel free to
open an issue on [GitHub](https://github.com/andrew-fuller) or reach out directly.
